title: Cron & Repeat
description: Schedule recurring jobs with cron expressions

blocks.text: Hound supports recurring jobs via the repeat.pattern option. A cron expression makes a job self-schedule — after each successful execution, Hound re-enqueues it for the next occurrence automatically.
Set repeat.pattern on a handler registration or a JobDefinition to enable cron scheduling:
The first execution is scheduled when hound.start() is called. Subsequent executions are scheduled automatically after each completion.
repeat.catchUp controls what happens to a missed cron tick when the worker restarts or the Reaper resurrects a stalled cron entry. Default is false.

rows: Missed ticks are skipped; only the next natural fire runs. Use for sync, cache, prebuild — running twice is worse than skipping once.
The missed tick is fired on recovery (backfill once). Use for financial, compliance, reporting — every tick must run.

blocks.text: catchUp is cron-only. Setting it on a non-cron job (no repeat.pattern) throws at hound.on() and emit(). Non-cron jobs are always recovered by the Reaper.

Two guards enforce the semantics: (1) the restart preflight in start() rewrites stale :delayed entries to the next natural fire when catchUp is false; (2) the consumer-side guard in #processJob skips claimed entries older than one cron interval and reschedules. The existing cron-exec lock (keyed by jobId + delayUntil) is preserved as belt-and-braces dedup against parallel claims of the same tick.
You can also set a repeat pattern at emit time rather than at handler registration. This is useful for per-user or per-tenant scheduled jobs:
Always use a stable custom ID with per-emit repeat jobs. Without a fixed ID, each restart would enqueue an additional recurring job instance.
